Strategic Communications Subcommittee Report
Committee: Strategic Communications
Meeting date: November 8, 2016
Chair: Candy deCsipkes
Committee Members in attendance: Candy deCsipkes, Becky Foley, Lindsay Sterling
Agenda Items and Discussion:
There was a discussion about the question and answer period (Public Comment Period) that will occur
before the Board meeting on November 9, 2016:
Key points:
The Board wants to ensure that all of the stakeholders are heard.
The Board seeks to make the decision that is best for the district as a whole.
The Board wants to build unity and pride.
The Strategic Communications Committee created the survey in order for stakeholders to vote and
have their voice heard.
We wanted to give the public the opportunity to benefit from a discussion about the pros and cons of
naming versus renaming. Please fill out the survey even if you provide public comment during one of
the Board meetings.
Going forward:
Two other opportunities to give public comment will be available in Durham (November 30th) and
Pownal (December 14th). If you can’t make one, come to the other one.
Superintendent’s office will tabulate results.
Board results will be discussed at the Dec. 14th meeting.
Board will determine next steps.
The Board will vote and make a final determination on whether to rename or not at some point.
Submitted by: Becky Foley
Regional School Unit No. 5 17 West St., Freeport, ME 04032 Telephone: 865-0928x5

Strategic Communications Subcommittee Report
Committee: Strategic Communications
Meeting date: September 23, 2016
Chair: Candy deCsipkes
Committee Members in attendance: Kate Brown, Candy deCsipkes, Becky Foley, Lindsay Sterling,
Valy Steverlynck
Also in attendance: Naomi Ledbetter
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Work on surveys for Unity and Pride Project
The updated Communication Suggestions version was shared and finalized. Minor revisions were
made. This will be distributed to the administrative team next week, with Becky highlighting the ones
she feels is most critical.
Satisfaction Survey: This was reviewed by the B Team in September, and is now ready to be
formatted. The committee decided that it would be given some time in the spring
Exit Survey: Feedback was gathered from the B Team meeting in September. Administrators believe
that it needs to be rewritten with a more neutral tone, so that feedback is solicited whether it is positive
or negative from parents. Becky will present a rewritten draft at the next Strategic Communications’
Meeting.
Unity & Pride Survey: The committee revised the Unity & Pride Survey. The draft will be presented to
the Board on September 28th for feedback.
Cost Estimates for Renaming: Becky shared some preliminary data on costs that would be incurred if
the high school were renamed. Becky solicited feedback from the Board members on how much detail
and materials are needed when presenting to the Board on this subject.
Next meeting will focus on finalizing the Unity & Pride Survey and outlining a process to be utilized
for decision making around whether to rename the high school or not.
Meeting dates for the 2016-2017 school year

TITLE: May 12, 2016 and May 26, 2016 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

Strategic Communication Committee Meetings
Meeting Dates: May 12, 2016 and May 26, 2016
Committee: Strategic Communications
Chair: Candy deCsipkes
May 12, 2016 Meeting
Present: Kate Brown, Candy deCsipkes, Lindsay Sterling, Valy Steverlynk and Mike Lafortune
At this meeting the group discussed/brainstormed about an exit survey and a "branding/marketing"
plan. They talked briefly about a general satisfaction survey.
"Branding/Marketing":
A suggested name for this effort could be "Unity and Pride Project" (UPP). A possible goal for the
effort might be: to create pride, unity, engagement, excitement and a sense of belonging in our
school district. Possible strategy might be: to have students, staff, parents, and community
members explore the district's identity; including who we are, what we're great at, what we're known
for and what we'd like to be known for. The process could include having a staff member assigned
the task of overseeing this effort; having students explore the history of the towns in the district and
visit schools; having each school explore their identity; holding community meetings; and looking at
logos, names, etc. -- visual identity. The group visited the renaming of the high school. THIS IS
ALL VERY PRELIMINARY AND STILL UNDER DISCUSSION.
Much of this meeting was a visit from Nancy Drolet to talk with the group about her efforts in this
area.
Exit survey:
Below are the results of the second draft for the exit survey. Discussion at this meeting moved us
forward toward developing what's below at the next meeting.
May 26, 2016 Meeting
Present: Kate Brown, Candy deCsipkes, Lindsay Sterling, Valy Steverlynk and Mike Lafortune
The focus of this meeting was reviewing a draft of the exit survey and continued discussion about
this document. The categories in the survey would be:
o Moving
o Draw from other schools
Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West St., Freeport, ME 04032 Telephone: 865-0928x5 E-mail: mcmanusg@rsu5.org

o Parent's concerns not addressed
o Student's academic needs not met
o Student's GT and/or Special Ed needs not met
o Concerns about academic subjects
o Concerns about extracurricular activities
o Concerns about athletics
o Student's social/emotional needs not met
o Concerns about discipline/behavior issues
o Safety concerns
o Ineffective communication from teachers and/or administrators
o Few opportunities for parent participation
There are further check-offs under some of the categories. There would be a section for open-ended
comments about reasons for leaving and suggestions for improvement. The survey would be
anonymous and handled through the Central Office.
Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West St., Freeport, ME 04032 Telephone: 865-0928x5 E-mail: mcmanusg@rsu5.org

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/b0d47a72-b2b6-4807-ba9d-62b6c5d4370e
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/strategic-communications-committee
TITLE: November 5, 2015 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

Date: 11/10/15
Committee: Strategic Communications
Chair: Candice Decsipkes
In attendance: Candy, Lindsay, Valy, Kate, Mike, Ed
Meeting Date: 11/5/15
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Committee Role
Good discussion around the role and responsibilities of this committee. General agreement that this committee
should get information needs to Ed and let his office handle specifics. Committee should play a more advisory
role in communications.
Example: Committee recommends that we do information piece about our high school in November around
recruitment time. Ed takes this information and organizes communications.
Example: Committee recommends that we provide budget information. Ed has plans to create budget booklets
that that go to each citizen.
2. Current Proficiency Article
Referring to item one, the committee does not feel it should be drafting and editing communications.
Ed will review latest version and make recommendation to Candy for publication.
3. Tri-County Board Column will terminate.
Ed will be forwarding his weekly district updates to this newspaper.
4. Website
Will be getting major overhaul in spring.
5. Leadership Group

Meeting Date: September 30, 2015

Planning for the Year
● The group discussed the timing of the State of Schools Publication and would like to
align this with communication around the School Board’s adopted FY 17 budget.
The committee discussed needing to communicate “the narrative” around the
proposed budget.
● The committee discussed making use of the Tri-Town Weekly column and
established dates for submission of articles (10/9, 10/30, 11/20, 12/11, 1/8 1/29, 2/19,
3/4 , 3/25, 4/15, 5/6, 5/27) and initial articles. October 9 will be an “opening of
schools” article; October 30th an effort to communicate “reporting student progress
in a proficiency based environment” in grades 6-12; November 20th on high school
offerings in an effort to capture interest of 8th graders and on December 11th an
update on the high school project.
● The committee would like town officials to receive newsletters and Superintendent
Updates to Staff.
● The committee would like to see committee members visit various council and select
board meetings (2x per year?).
● Discussed taking advantage of Tri-Town 1 page insert.
Next Meeting: Thursday, November 4, 2015 @ 4 pm.

Regional School Unit No. 5
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic Communications Subcommittee Report
Date: June 4, 2014
Committee: Strategic Communications
Chair: Candice Decsipkes
In attendance: Candice Decsipkes, Kathryn Brown, Valy Steverlynck, Joe Makley
Meeting Date: May 28, 2014
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Plan Revisions:
• Pos Post Secondary comparison data is now provided by the Maine DOE
• Cross-curricular Standards are now being piloted at all grade levels
• Grade 2 Fountas and Pinnel Reading level scores are available
• Writing prompts baseline data is available for grades 7&8.
• AP Participation data is available for years 2008-2009 through 2012-2013.
Cross-curricular Standards Pilot report out:
The Cross-curricular Skills are being piloted in all buildings this year. Feedback is being
collected from each group for final adjustments to procedures/protocols, to be determined in
June. Professional development will be conducted in August. Policy IKE, IKF (that reference
the new standards) have been submitted to the board and are in 1st Read. The new standards will
be a component of the shift to a proficiency-based system for the class of 2018.
Writing Assessment Report out:
Joe Makley reported that the writing assessment for 8th grade (class of 2018) was completed with
a baseline performance of 62% achieving a score of proficient or distinguished. The new local
assessment is aligned with adopted curriculum (including state standards,) and will be a regular
feature of writing instruction.
Superintendent’s email
The committee agreed to make a recommendation to the superintendent that official
communications from the superintendent’s office be sent out from the superintendents’ email
address, rather than that of the administrative assistant.
Improving communication
There was a discussion of ideas to improve communication, including: revising a list of current
communications methods, establishing a new community opinion survey, and enriching the
format of town government workshops and budget meetings.
Press release recommended? No

Regional School Unit No. 5
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic Communications Subcommittee Report
Date: March 11, 2014
Committee: Strategic Communications
Chair: Candice Decsipkes
In attendance: Shannon Welsh, Candice Decsipkes, Kathryn Brown, Valy Steverlynck, Joe
Makley
Meeting Date: March 6, 2014
Agenda Items and Discussion:
State of the Schools Draft Report
Joe Makley shared preliminary copies of the State of the School report. On the NECAP, RSU5
students are continuing to perform well above the state average and approaching regional
averages in reading and math. Both 3rd and 5th grade students matched or exceeded the regional
average in math. Grade 8 students performed well against the state average, but somewhat below
the regional average in math. On the Maine High School Assessment, RSU5 students continue
to score well above the state averages in all subjects, and somewhat below the regional average,
with the largest gap in math. (Secondary math is an improvement focus at RSU5.) The number
of low-income students at RSU5 increased slightly in 2013, and remains more than double that
of the higher scoring regional schools.
Dine and Discuss Planning:
The format will be similar to last year with topic signs at tables, and one RSU5 representative at
each table to collect feedback. Soup and sandwiches will be served. Dates:
• April 9th in Pownal
• April 30th in Durham
• May 7th in Freeport
The next meeting of the Strategic Communications Committee is scheduled for May 29, 2014 at
4:30 p.m. at the Central Office

TITLE: October 3, 2013 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West Street, Freeport, Maine 04032
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic Communications Subcommittee Report
Date: October 7, 2013
Committee: Strategic Communications
Chair: Candace Decsipkes
In attendance: Kathryn Brown, Valy Steverlynck
Meeting Date: October 3, 2013
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Plan update:
Cross-curricular standards for work and college readiness:
These standards apply to more than one subject, for example: “Self Management/ Complete work on time.”
Mast Landing and Durham are scoring a set of the standards at grades K-5. Grades 6-8 are now scoring a set of
these standards for all students. The high school has a group of 12 teachers scoring for the new standards to
provide feedback and recommendations for full implementation in September 2014. The result will be clearer
and more consistent information for students, parents and teachers about these skills, which were identified in
the RSU5 Strategic Framework as critical for success in college and careers. For more information, see the
online curriculum resource: http://rsu5curriculum.blogspot.com/2013/06/cross-curricular-standards.html
7/8 Grade Writing Prompts:
The Teachers College Writing Workshop assessment format will be used for the writing prompt at grades 7-8
this spring, and for the lower grades going forward. Base line data will be available after the spring
assessment. This annual measure, established in the strategic plan, will provide reliable information on how
RSU5 students (individuals, schools and the district as a whole) improve over time.
Standards-based System:
Status report:
RSU5 elementary schools have been using standards for many years, and next fall will have report card
information available on line across the RSU. Middle School classes at Durham currently use standards as
reference for traditional grades. Freeport Middle School is piloting a transition to standards-based reporting
this year for all classes. Freeport High School is piloting this year with a limited number of teachers. Results
of the pilot will inform planning for the changes necessary to provide “proficiency-based” pathway/s for
entering 9th grade students next year. (The class of 2018 will be the first subject to Maine’s new proficiency
requirement for a high school diploma.) Middle and high school students will continue to receive grades. The
changes have more to do with improved curricula and criteria for the awarding of grades and credit, along with
increased flexibility and choice for students.
Communications plan :
• There will be three board workshops this year focusing on different aspects of the transition to a
standards-based system: 11/06/2013 (Overview and K-5,) 01/08/2014 (Middle School Programs,) and
03/12/2014 (High school redesign/innovation/student choice/proficiency-based diploma)
• The State of the Schools Report (March 2014) will have additional information on the standards
transition.
• There will be 3 “Dine and Discuss” information sessions (April/May 2014.)
• For more information, questions may be addressed to the RSU 5 online curriculum discussion:
http://rsu5curriculum.blogspot.com/
Press release recommended? No
Submitted by: Joe Makley

Dine and Discuss sessions:
The committee discussed the three “Dine and Discuss” sessions, held in each of the towns on 4/10,
4/24, and 5/1. There were a variety of conversations at these events. It was decided that this format
provided more opportunity for discussion than the previous format of a single large event, so it should
continue next year, with an opportunity for participants to share and summarize the feedback they
received. Also, more opportunities should be sought to engage the public on school issues.
Strategic Framework Revisions:
• Graduation and college attendance rates are now provided by the Maine DOE
• Work and College Readiness title clarification… change to” Cross-curricular Standards for
Work and College Readiness.” Status changed to “Skills identified, rubrics complete”
• Co-curricular participation clarified to read: “total number of students for each of the two
applicable categories…” Status changed to “Data is available.”
• Advanced Placement participation… Status changed to “data is available.”
Joe Makley provided status reports on two pilot projects:
• Work and College Readiness (changes reflected above)
• Writing Prompt at grades 7-8 (First testing/scoring round complete, anchor papers chosen,
testing and scoring protocols refined, on track for base-line data report after second round in
the Spring 2014.)

Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West Street, Freeport, Maine 04032
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic Communications Subcommittee Report
Date: October 15, 2012
Committee: Strategic Communications
Chair: Jane Blais
Meeting Dates: October 4, 2012
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Plan Update (Career/College Readiness):
Joe Makley provided a status report on the Work and College Readiness pilot, in the context of transitioning to a
standards-based system. Social Studies teachers will be given the opportunity of piloting the WCR assessments
this year along with their revised curriculum units. Baseline data will be collected in school year 2013-2014.
The benefits to parents and students will be:
• Improved feedback on important knowledge and skills
• More consistent expectations from teachers
• Less variability from teacher to teacher in how different elements are combined for a grade.
The benefits for teachers will be:
• A clear way to prioritize key skills and knowledge across different subjects and grade spans
• Improved student accountability for work habits, etc.
• More consistent expectations for grading
Education Forum Planning:
Options for this year’s Community Forum were discussed. It was decided to postpone a decision whether to hold
the public forum, since attendance by members of the public has been limited. Alternative methods of dialogue and
obtaining feedback from the public may prove more effective than a one-evening event. The Strategic
Communications Committee will make a final decision at our meeting in January.
Next Strategic Communications Meeting: January 17, 2013 @ 4:30 p.m. at the Central Office

Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West Street, Freeport, Maine 04032
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic/Communications Subcommittee Report
Date: May 31, 2012
Committee: Strategic/Communications
Chair: Jane Blais
Meeting Dates: May 31, 2012
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Framework
Joe Makley, Curriculum Director, joined the group to review the Work and College Readiness materials
developed over the course of this school year. The Standards have been created in draft form and reviewed by
staff members. The Standards are supported by draft Measurement Topics and Scoring Guides. These will be
finalized over the summer for Board approval at the beginning of the next school year.
Following a review of these materials, members supported the recommended change to the Strategic Framework
to reflect that these will be piloted in 2012-2013 with baseline data gathered in 2013-2014. The assessment
measures from the work and college readiness standards will be demonstration based instead of solely focused
on exhibitions. This change to the Strategic Framework will also be recommended to the Board for
consideration.
Additional changes to the document are recommended for Fountas & Pinnell to reflect our current practice of
using this assessment in grades K-8 and in the District Writing Prompt to reflect an implementation timeline
similar to Work and College Readiness.
These changes will go to the RSU5 Board at their meeting on June 13th for consideration and adoption. This
will set the stage for work in the next school year.
Next Strategic/Communications Meeting: TBD

Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West Street, Freeport, Maine 04032
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic/Communications Subcommittee Report
Date: October 6, 2011
Committee: Strategic/Communications
Chair: Jane Blais
Meeting Dates: October 6, 2011
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Framework:
A timeline was presented for creation and publication of the second annual State of the Schools Report. The
annual Forum is scheduled for April 2, 2010 and discussion was held regarding whether we should have
someone of Angus King’s caliber speak on national and international educational initiatives. Committee
members would like to have a speaker or an elected official such as the Education Commissioner, the Governor,
or student presentations. We will research options and report back on a presentation. Following the more global
presentation, citizens would participate in focus group discussions regarding action that might be taken as a
result of the Report, the Strategic Plan, and the presentation. Discussion also centered on whether to provide a
pre-forum dinner that would benefit a group such as Project Graduation.
Committee members discussed adding items to the State of the Schools Report to include some of the actions
taken as a result of last year’s report using the Strategic Plan commitments to frame the information. There was
also discussion about creating a summary of the results to make it easier to digest all of the information
provided.
General Communication Efforts:
The Board’s goals contain several communication efforts. One is to contact parent groups and municipal
officers at the beginning of the school year and as needed throughout the year. Committee members agreed to
act as a contact for their community and touch base with each organization. The contacts will be made over the
next few months with a deadline of December.
A second element is to work toward a common municipal elections date for the RSU Board. Following a
lengthy discussion, members agreed to ask municipalities to hold a June election for RSU Board vacancies.
This will create a full Board for the start of a new school year with members attending an orientation and Retreat
prior to their first business meeting. The RSU holds a budget vote at that time so there would be no additional
cost to municipalities for the election. Prior to contacting municipal officers, we need to research the steps to
changing the impacted towns’ election dates and the potential costs of such a request. After attaining that
information, members will approach the town officers between Thanksgiving and Christmas.
If elections are held in June, members would like to recommend the Board complete their self-evaluation prior
to the end of May to gain input from all current members.
Next Strategic/Communications Meeting: January 12, 2012 @ 4:30 p.m. at the Central Office
Press release recommended? No
Submitted by: Shannon L. Welsh

Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West Street, Freeport, Maine 04032
Durham Freeport Pownal
Joint Meeting of Strategic Planning and Communications
Subcommittee Report
Date: March 3, 2011
Committee: Communications
Chair: Jane Blais
Meeting Dates: March 3, 2011
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Framework Report Out Planning:
The committee met with Ken Templeton from Great Schools to review a draft of the information for
our upcoming State of the Schools Report. The committee finalized the text and graphs for this report
of our progress against the indicators outlined in the Strategic Framework. The report will be mailed to
every household in our three communities. The document should arrive at households during the week
of March 21st.
The committee also discussed the RSU5 Education Forum scheduled for March 29, 2011 at 6:30 pm in
the FPAC. Academic and financial indicators outlined in the Strategic Framework will be used to
provide an accounting of our performance at this point in time. The Forum will provide a community
discussion of what’s been accomplished, areas to celebrate, and what goals we should establish as we
look to the future. Invitations will be extended through the “Delivering on the Mission message,
school newsletters, the town cable stations, and through press releases.
Next Communications Meeting: April 7, 2011 @ 3:30
Press release recommended? Yes, for the Education Forum
Submitted by: Shannon L. Welsh

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/a08683a0-ba31-4896-9628-78e613f39a20
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/strategic-communications-committee
TITLE: October 7, 2010 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

Regional School Unit No. 5
17 West Street, Freeport, Maine 04032
Durham Freeport Pownal
Strategic Planning Subcommittee Report
Date: October 8, 2010
Committee: Strategic Planning
Meeting Date: October 7, 2010
Members Present: Eric Dube, Peter Murray, Jane Blais, and Shannon Welsh
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Strategic Plan Update:
Members received a report on the design of our October 8th Professional Learning Day. The
focus of the day is to provide detailed information about the Strategic Framework and time for
the development of building, team and individual implementation plans. All professional staff
will meet to hear the planning process for creating plans based on student literacy data that will
move us toward attainment of the district mission and the performance commitments outlined in
the Strategic Framework.
Completed building and team level plans will be presented to the Board of Directors at their
meeting on October 27th and posted on our websites. A report out on attainment of the goals and
plans will be provided as part of the State of the Schools report in February/March. This
provides only a 6 month window for action so plans will need to be realistic to meet this
timeframe. Following the State of the Schools report on updated data, the planning process will
begin again for the 2011-2012 school year.
Strategic Plan members discussed the importance of this process and the impact to student
learning. They will review initial data in January and finalize the State of the Schools report.
Members will recommend any changes to the Framework or process based on the work done
over the next 6 months.
Next Meeting: January 6, 2011
Press release recommended? Not at this time.
Submitted by: Shannon L. Welsh, Superintendent

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/02888bc6-f880-4bd8-8015-14f9a89258c1
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/strategic-communications-committee
TITLE: WCRTaskForcePlanTimeline
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

RSU No. 5 K-12 Work and College Readiness Task Force Work Plan and Time Line
December 2 (9:00-11:30) Employer Feedback Meeting
• Solicit essential work readiness skills from business, non-profit, and municipal employers
• Recommendations will inform the K-12 Educator Task Force’s work
December 20 (8:30 – 3:00) K-12 Educator Task Force Organizational Meeting
• Use December 2 information and existing work and college readiness standards both in and outside
the district to draft RSU No. 5’s work and college readiness standards.
• Plan stakeholder feedback process.
• Refine group work plan, outcomes, and products for the year.
• Determine the rubric format.
• Identify quality and content criteria to guide rubric development.
January 3-19 Feedback
Feedback on identified standards (electronic, in staff meetings and early release, in the A-Team,
from the December 2 group in writing).
January 24 (tentative)
College readiness focus group (college representatives and high school teachers and guidance
staff).
January 27 (8:30 – 3:00) Task Force Work Session
• Incorporate feedback.
• Refine rubric development process.
• Begin rubric development in work groups.
February 16, March 3, March 17 (8:30 – 11:30 and 12:30 – 3:30)
(Task Force members will have the option to condense this into one full day.)
Rubric Development Work Sessions
March 24 (12:30 – 3:30) Task Force Work Session
• Share and refine rubric drafts.
• Identify existing assessment opportunities.
• Refine rubric piloting process with existing assessments.
• Identify opportunities for community members to participate in refining assessments and being
part of juried exhibitions.
March 31 (12:30 – 3:30) Additional Task Force Work Session (if needed)
April 26 (3:00 – 4:30) Task Force Check-in Meeting
May 25 (12:30 – 3:30) Task Force Work Session
• Share experiences using rubrics with existing assessments.
• Identify assessments for use or adaptation.
• Revise rubrics as necessary in work groups.
June 21, 22 (8:30 – 12:00), June 23 (8:30 – 2:30), August 18 (8:30 – 12:00) Task Force Work Sessions
• Use the rubric testing experience, the curriculum, and the Strategic Plan Framework performance
indicators to create an assessment framework (pacing, format, and draft scoring guides) for the
standards.
• Refine/develop appropriate assessments at each grade span.
• Identify instructional implications of the rubrics and the standards.
• Draft recommended grade span implementation plans for phase-in of standards and assessments
(A-Team discussion and final plans sometime before August 26).
Task Force Member Meeting Time Commitment: School Year 10-11: Two full release days, four
(possibly five) ½ day release times (group could decide to condense three of the ½ days to one full day),
one after school meeting. Summer Work: Three ½ days, one full day
Task Force Roles and Responsibilities: Provide information from his or her school as needed; explain
Task Force work in staff meetings and facilitate school-level feedback processes as needed; participate in
rubric development work groups; pilot rubrics (and/or enlist others to pilot rubrics) between March and
May; review work via email between meetings and work sessions.

Reflection
Progress toward enhancing the integration of technology has been slower than anticipated. While some improvement has
been made in fostering collaboration between the technology team and educators, there are still significant gaps in the
support we have been able to provide. I have struggled with time and bandwidth constraints, and the rest of our team has

more of a background in traditional IT, hardware support, and technology infrastructure, which has limited our ability to
maintain a consistent presence in classrooms and provide the level of ongoing support needed for educators to fully
leverage digital tools.
Although we have made some efforts to offer professional development opportunities, the reach and impact have been
insufficient. Many staff members have expressed a need for more tailored, in-depth training and guidance, but the
technology department simply hasn’t had the capacity to meet these demands.
Looking ahead, it’s clear that much more work is needed to make technology a truly embedded part of the instructional
process. As we work to improve district infrastructure and security over this summer, I hope to further prioritize increasing
my presence with educators, not just in terms of training but through consistent, proactive engagement in the classroom.

Technology Goals 2024-2025 Review
Department of Technology
Goal Strategies and Action Steps Timeline Evidence of Effectiveness
(Responsibility)
Expand Newsletter Creation and 2024-2025 School Year • Develop clear, engaging, and
communication from Contributions relevant write-ups for various
the technology newsletter editions, focusing on
department for topics that align with district

and to foster

• Solicit feedback from newsletter

improve the relevance and quality
of the contributions.
• Plan regular Technology-only
newsletter for internal
stakeholders.
Reflection

Progress toward expanding communication from the technology department has been mixed this year. While we have made
strides in increasing transparency and outreach, much of this work was reactive rather than proactive. The PowerSchool
data breach earlier in the year required an immediate and sustained communication effort, and while it ultimately
strengthened our connection with stakeholders and reinforced the importance of clear, timely updates, it was not the
intentional, forward-facing communication I had originally planned to implement.
Outside of that incident, consistent communication with staff and the broader school community has improved, but remains
a challenge. Competing priorities and limited time have made it difficult to develop and maintain regular, proactive
communication channels. Although I’ve taken steps to be more visible and responsive, particularly around security-related
issues, there is still room for improvement when it comes to broader professional growth messaging and stakeholder
engagement.
I hope to build on the communication practices that emerged during the crisis response and develop a more structured
approach that supports both transparency and ongoing professional dialogue. With greater focus and planning, I believe we
can create more predictable, accessible communication that better supports the needs of our community.

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/7a48d38e-4594-4d01-8e0c-37a97d8e90dd
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/standing-committees/facilities-and-operations
TITLE: March 11, 2026 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Facilities and Operations Committee
Wednesday, March 11, 2026
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School - Library
Minutes
Committee Members Present: Michelle Ritcheson, Karen Breer
Committee Members Absent: Kelly Sink
Administrators in Attendance: Tom Gray, Superintendent; Glen Reynolds, Director of Facilities; Kelly
Wentworth, Director of Finance and Human Resources
Guest: Molly Meyer, FHS Tennis Coach, Mark Dohle Pownal Resident
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Meeting called to order at 5:04pm
1. FHS Tennis Courts Update- Director Reynolds updated the Committee that the site
survey is underway and the core sampling would be happening in the next few weeks..
Director Reynolds went over the preliminary layouts of the current 3 court and a
possible 4 court replacement options. The Committee then reviewed and discussed the
early concept estimates for both asphalt and post tension courts. Tennis Coach, Molly
Meyers, gave an update on additional steps needed during the USTA grant application
process which include a technical review of the original set of drawings and a final
review of all bid documents by USTA before bidding can commence. This process would
have implications on the timeline. Director Reynolds was asked to provide additional
information on the layout of the courts as well as the possibility of a curtain instead of
fencing between the courts.
2. Energy Audit Phase 1 Project Update- Director Reynolds updated the Committee that the
lighting vendor is currently working at Freepoort Middle School. The next location for
this project is Mast Landing School. Director Reynolds also shared that once the lighting
project at Mast Landing is complete the vendor will be going to Freeport High School.
This is a change from the original schedule due to the length of time the lighting project
will take and the availability of the FPAC. Director Reynolds also reported that EMC was
preparing an updated Phase 2 Projects Listing.
3. Facility Study Update- Director Reynolds shared with the Committee a draft of the
proposed Facility Study Request for Qualifications (RFQ) document. Superintendent Tom
Gray and Director of Finance and Human Resources Kelly Wentworth shared the overall
concept of the Facility Study process. This would begin with a review of all current

facilities, a determination of current conditions, an educational analysis of needs, a
demographic/enrollment study, Community feedback listening sessions, then suggest
and rank possible space reuse to meet educational and economic conditions in the next
ten years. Committee Member Karen Breer shared that she has access to some other
Facility Study RFQs and would email them to Superintendent Gray who can then share
them with the Committee. Committee members are asked to share any suggested
revisions and/or changes prior to the next meeting so that the Committee can review
any revisions to the RFQ at the next meeting on April 8, 2026.
Motion to adjourn was made by Michelle Ritcheson second by Karen Breer at 6:13 p.m.
Next meeting dates:
April 8, 2026
May 6, 2026
June 3, 2026

replacements listed in the plan in order to obtain the best pricing. Director of Finance Kelly

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Facilities and Operations Committee
Wednesday, October 8, 2025
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School - Library
Minutes
Committee Members Present: Michelle Ritcheson, Maura Pillsbury
Committee Members Absent: Kelly Sink
Administrators in Attendance: Tom Gray, Superintendent; Glen Reynolds, Director of Facilities;
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance and Human Resources
Guests: Jason Chisholm, Field Maintenance Supervisor
Agenda Items and Discussion:
Meeting called to order at 5:17 p.m.
Motion was made by Michelle Ritcheson seconded by Maura Pillsbury for the addition of item 6 on
the agenda.
1. Update on Board Facilities Tour. - Director Reynolds updated the Committee on the scheduling
of a full school board tour of our buildings. Michelle Ritcheson suggested that Ginny would
send an email to the full Board with some dates for consideration.
2. Update on ADA egress. Director Reynolds updated the committee on the need to have an
ADA automatic door opener installed at the front entrance at FHS as there is currently not one.
The automatic door opener is scheduled to be installed over December break. Director
Reynolds updated the committee that he is working on a short term solution for ramps on
various doors at FHS, while working on a long term solution through the budget process.
3. Update on Phase 2 Energy Audit projects- Director Reynolds updated the committee on the
Phase 2 projects. Phase 2 projects will be discussed at a joint Finance/Facilities committee
meeting scheduled for 12/10/25.
4. Update on request for proposals for tennis court replacement- Director Reynolds updated the
committee that we have the scope of work completed. Once that is reviewed by the Director of
Finance & HR we can then send it to the newspapers for posting. This should be completed by
the end of next week.

5. Turf field update- Field Maintenance Supervisor Jason Chisholm updated the committee on the
turf field G-MAX test results. Jason reported that the field passed. It was agreed upon by all
that we continue the G-MAX testing every August. Jason mentioned the tentative repair dates
for the turf repairs and track striping. At this time the turf repairs are scheduled for 10/14-17th
weather permitting. The track striping is scheduled for 10/10-13th weather permitting.
6. Update on Durham Community School Automation System- Director Reynolds updated the
committee that the current building automation system at Durham Committee School is in need
of replacement. This system runs the heating and ventilation system and is currently
scheduled for replacement in phase two of the energy audit upgrades. Director of Finance
Wentworth identified the Capital Reserve Account as a possible funding source. Michelle
Ritcheson made a motion, seconded by Maura Pillsbury to approve up to $55,000 from Capital
Reserves for the building automation system upgrades at Durham Community School. The
Board of Directors will need to approve this request at their next meeting.
Motion to adjourn was made by Michelle Ritcheson second by Maura Pillsbury at 5:59 p.m.
Meeting dates:
November 5, 2025
December 10,
January 14, 2026

TYPE: Finalsite-PDF

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Tom Gray, Superintendent of Schools Erica Mazzeo, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Facilities and Operations Committee
Wednesday, September 10, 2025
5:00 p.m. - Freeport High School - Library
Minutes
Committee Members Present: Michelle Ritcheson, Kelly Sink
Committee Members Absent: Maura Pillsbury
Administrators in Attendance: Tom Gray, Superintendent; Glen Reynolds, Director of Facilities;
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance and Human Resources
Guests: Jason Chisholm, Field Maintenance Supervisor
Agenda Items and Discussion:
The meeting started at 5:10 p.m.
Michelle Ritcheson made a motion to appoint Kelly Sink as Chair. Vote: 2 - 0
1. Meeting called to order at 5:06 p.m.
2. Update new leach field/playground at Pownal- Director Reynolds updated the committee on
the newly completed leach field system as well as the newly installed playground equipment.
Director Reynolds also updated the committee on the reason the original swingsets were
removed as well as the design and cost of the new swingsets.
3. Update on phase 1 projects- Director Reynolds updated the committee on the phase one
projects as well as the tentative start date for the lighting project to begin.
4. Update on vehicle & grounds equipment replacement- Director Reynolds updated the
Committee on the vehicle and equipment that was purchased this summer. He also included
the reason why a switch was made and other equipment for the following year was purchased
this past summer.
5. Update on request for proposals for tennis courts- Director Reynolds updated the committee
that we are still working on putting the request for proposals together. Once completed Director
Reynolds will update them.
6. Turf field update- Field Maintenance Supervisor Jason Chisholm updated the committee on the
status of the turf field as well as the track. Jason explained that we are on year 8 of the field
and track and we are also out of warranty. The Nike Grind from 8 years ago (original
installation) needs replacement - losing its pliability. The turf itself has perhaps 4-6 years left.

Discussed possible solutions to continue to maintain the field so that it reaches its anticipated
lifespan. Jason recommends that beginning this year, we G-Max test the field annually to
ensure concussion safety. This would also be a way to continually test the safety of the field.
Jason also mentioned the need for maintenance at the edges of the turf to ensure it is properly
pinned and with proper drainage (curbing). Director of Finance Kelly Wentworth shared
financial information about possible funding.
Michelle Ritcheson made a motion, seconded by Kelly Sink to approve up to $25,000 for
maintenance of the track and field from Capital Reserve Fund. Vote: 2 - 0
The Board of Directors will need to approve this request at their next meeting.
The meeting adjourned at 6:20 p.m.
Meeting dates:
October 8, 2025
November 5, 2025
January 14, 2026
February 11, 2026
March 11, 2026
April 8, 2026
May 6, 2026

Freeport High School Building Advisory Committee
Tuesday, May 8, 2018
FHS Cafeteria
7:30 a.m.
Minutes
Attendance: Michelle Lickteig, John Simoneau, Michelle Ritcheson, Becky Foley, David
Steckler, David Watts, Kevin Nadeau, Craig Sickels, Becky Foley
The Committee discussed how to use the remaining funds of about $198,000. The committee
prioritized the requests in the following order. Estimated costs will be checked before placing the
items on an agenda for the Board to consider.
Contingency funds: $198,000
Priorities & estimates from Jen, Becky, Dennis
1. Security ($12,000 total) $12,000
1. ADA buzzer on the front ramp
2. Second FPAC door
3. Door sensor panel
4. Timer on the ski door
2. Lighting on side of gym $1200
3. Repairing cafeteria footstools and counters $5000
4. Classroom needs $27,500
1. Replace all old furniture in classrooms
2. Lights in JMG room
5. Paving teacher lot $55,000
6. Theater storage: overhead door $800
7. Bulletin boards for cafeteria $500
8. Tennis court picnic tables $135/each $500
9. Clear Com System for FPAC - $10,550
10. Peace garden benches $400/each $2000
11. Re-tile bathroom floors in 300 wing $8000
12. Renovation plaque: $1700
13. Folding tables (15 @ $60) $900
14. FPAC needs:
A. LED Cyc Lights $7600
B. Crown PCC microphones $735
C. 3rd Row LED Backlights $3400
15. New bookshelves/casework in old wing classrooms $50,000
16. Hallway gated doors $???
Total: $185,100...without hallway gated doors
Last committee meeting will take place off site at a place TBD, possibly Gritty’s.

SOURCE: https://www.rsu5.org/fs/resource-manager/view/70c68561-2160-4719-9a1d-d45dd0c2c236
SECTION: /board-of-directors-and-policies/board-committees/advisory-committees/fhs-building-advisory-committee-archive
TITLE: March 1, 2017 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
March 1, 2017 at 7:30 a.m.
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Ben Jamo, Becky Foley, Superintendent/RSU 5,
Jen Gulko, Principal/FHS, Dennis Ouelette/Owner’s Rep., David Smail/FHS, Kim Lamarre/Committee
Member, Michelle Lickteig/RSU 5 Business Office/Human Resources, Kevin Nadeau, David Steckler,
and Suzanne Morin/PDT Architects
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order at 7:35 a.m.
2. The Building Committee read and reviewed the February 1, 2017 Building
Committee meeting notes. A motion was made by Becky Foley and
seconded by Dennis Ouelette to approve the meeting notes. The meeting
notes were approved unanimously.
3. Chairman Simoneau gave a construction update. He explained there was
an invoice requisition submitted with a current payment due of $119,050. It
will be reviewed at the next pay requisition meeting on March 7, 2017 by the
architect and construction team.
4. This invoice brings the percent complete up to 92.8%.
5. Dennis Ouelette gave a brief construction update. He explained the work
was going well. Most of the framing was done in the administrative office
area, as well as the new art rooms and new IT work/conference rooms.
Dennis offered to give Committee members a quick peek at the work after
the meeting.
6. Dennis explained work was “on-going” in terms of metal stud framing,
electrical, conduit, wiring and HVAC ductwork. He also explained the
windows were kept on the north side of the cafeteria to let more natural light
into the art rooms.
7. Dennis explained that asbestos tile and mastic had been removed in the two
major hallways at the 1955 building over the February vacation break.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 4

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
A Committee member asked about the strong odor that was permeating the
hallways. He explained that staff and students were concerned the odors
were not toxic or harmful. Dennis and Becky Foley explained they had
reviewed the MSDS chemical sheets. There does not appear to be any
harmful side effects as a result of using the chemical strippers, although the
odor was strong. Dennis explained fans had been used and doors will be
kept open to try to air out the hallways.
8. David Smail shared a small bag of asbestos tile samples which he found
under a bank of lockers. He asked if there was still asbestos and chemical
residue under the lockers that should be considered harmful. Becky and
Dennis stated that the asbestos abatement contractors would have to come
back and do additional cleaning under the lockers. The loose,
miscellaneous scraps of tile would not pose a hazard because the tile and
mastic were not friable.
Dennis and Becky explained they would continue to try to air out the
hallways to reduce the odor as soon as possible. They will check with Mark
Coleman, the hazardous material hygienist, concerning any possible health
effects related to residue.
9. Lyndon explained that PDT was not directing that scope of work but it was
his understanding that the chemical odor is resulting from a chemical stripper
used to remove the black mastic from the flooring. The mastic is not friable
or dangerous; it is best removed chemically rather than grinding which would
make it friable and create dust in the environment.
10. John Simoneau gave a brief update on the track and field status. He
explained that the Committee had issued an RFQ to contractors requesting
qualifications from possible bidders.
John explained they would be going to the Project Review Board on the
evening of March 1st to get approval for the project. He explained the
Project Review Board had determined that they did not have authorization to
permit sports lighting in excess of poles higher than 20’. The Committee will
be working with the Project Review Board, Planner and Town Council to
have a special sports lighting ordinance implemented.
11. Traffic and parking had been raised as an issue. The Committee is
addressing events parking.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 4

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
12. There was a question asked if there were good contractors interested in
installing the track and field. John explained they expected to get several
good, qualified firms to submit RFQ’s who would want to participate in the
bidding.
13. Lyndon passed out a change proposal log showing recent approved items,
as well as upcoming items that PDT knew about. Lyndon explained that
Change Request #60RR and Change Request #89 were two items which
were in dispute between Sheridan Corporation and PDT Architects. Lyndon
explained PDT does not automatically approve every request from Sheridan
Corporation. We review all proposed change orders to determine if the price
seems reasonable. Lyndon explained Request #60RR was a disagreement
between the mason and PDT Architects concerning increasing the thickness
of the CMU wall at the Fitness Rooms. The mason was requesting $9,078;
PDT thought the price was closer to $1,500-$2,000.
Change Request #89 was at the knuckle between the gym, performing arts
center, new building and the 1955 building. Sheridan was requesting
$15,678. Lyndon explained that PDT had analyzed this and felt some of the
work was related to mistakes Sheridan had made. PDT thought the price
that was approvable was closer to $10,000.
Lyndon explained he wanted the Committee to know there were at least
these two outstanding items which were unresolved between Sheridan
Corporation and PDT Architects
14. The question was asked if the security cameras had all been installed.
Dennis explained all the inte rior cameras were now installed but there were
two or three exterior cameras which had not yet been installed.
15. There was a brief discussion about whether or not existing lockers in the old
building should remain or be taken out. This also included a brief discussion
of how many lockers are possibly needed. It was agreed that Dennis, Jen,
and Becky would report out on administration’s recommendation for how
many lockers should be left in the school and where they should be located
at the April meeting.
16. Jen asked Lyndon why the refrigerator had not been installed in the new
teacher’s room. Lyndon explained he will get an answer.
17. A question was asked if any more work was going to be done related to
putting lights on the high school signage. Comments were made that there
was some light on the west elevation facing Interstate 295. Perhaps there
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 3 of 4

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
would be enough residual lighting to light up the sign on that side of the
building.
18. Ben Jamo asked if it was possible to have lighting on the Holbrook Street
side of the building. Lyndon said PDT would consider lighting possibilities.
A recommendation from a Committee member to include looking at solar
floodlights that use small photovoltaic panels.
19. There was a discussion about whether the loading dock could be dressed up
and how it could be addressed. John and Dennis proposed to meet with
Lyndon to review it after the meeting.
20. The next meeting will be Wednesday, April 5, 2017 at 7:30 a.m. at Freeport
High School.
21. The meeting was adjourned at 8:15 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
November 2, 2016
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5,
Ben Jamo, David Watts/RSU 5, Dr. Becky Foley, Superintendent/RSU 5, David Smail/FHS Physics,
Kim LaMarre,Dennis Ouellete, Facilities Director/RSU 5, Tim Giddings, Kevin Nadeau and
Michelle Ritcheson
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The Building Committee started the meeting with a tour, which began at the
bus loop entrance at the dining commons. Hardhats were provided for all.
The tour was led by John Simoneau, Lyndon Keck and Brook Plummer.
2. The tour entered through the kitchen service door, went through the kitchen
and into the servery area. The group then went into the main dining
commons and reviewed lighting, colors and wall tile. The tour proceeded to
the health classroom and two fitness rooms where there was discussion
about proposed flooring and mirrors on the walls.
3. The tour then proceeded through the keyboarding classroom and into the
music/band classroom. Notice was taken of locations where Percent for Art
was going to be located in the corridors above the knapsack cubbies and
dining commons and hallway.
4. The tour proceeded up the central staircase to the second floor classroom
wing. On the second floor the tour went through past the teacher’s
workroom. It was asked if translucent screening was going to be put on the
glass. Lyndon explained no. The tour also went through several
classrooms, as well as toilet rooms. Questions were asked about floor tile
colors and patterns.
5. The group also looked at the second floor corridor where additional
photographs will be hung by the Art Subcommittee. The group then left the
building by going down the rear stairwell past student work area. The
Building Committee convened at Futures Lab 101.
6. The meeting began with John Simoneau summarizing the status of
completed work. The Committee was informed that pay requisition #13 was
approved in the amount of $582,188, which represented 82.6% completion
of the entire project.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 3

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
7. It was also informed that Change Order Summary #13 was approved in the
amount of $786. Change Order #13 included a credit of $4,500 for final
cleaning, additional toilet partitions at the boy’s M100 bathroom for $1,282,
and a construction team approval to push the dumpster pad 30’ to the east
for an additional cost of $2,432.
8. John Simoneau explained the Building Committee’s next meeting in
December would be important because they would need to finalize their list
of proposed improvements for the RSU 5 School Board. Additional numbers
needed include costs for the 100 Wing bathrooms and a review of previously
itemized potential adds.
9. There was a question regarding the renovated portions of the building. The
teachers were wondering when their rooms were going to be completely
finished so they would understand what they did and did not get.
10. Lyndon explained he didn’t know the schedule for IT upgrades being done
by the RSU. Additional renovation work on doors, Whiteboards, hallway
floor finish would not be complete until the spring. There is also additional
work which needs to be done in the spring such as removing metal,
demountable partitions and additional classrooms which did not receive
work. The teachers probably will not know the full extent of renovations in
some of their areas until September 2017.
11. There was a question about the proposed finish date. It was explained that
Sheridan thinks they will be finished the week of November 14th. Both John
and Lyndon stated they thought this was highly unlikely and would probably
be in the first or second week of December.
12. There was a concern expressed by the principal that the low flow flush toilets
were not always flushing properly. PDT to have Sheridan and Bennett
Engineering review the low flow toilets to determine what adjustments can
be made.
13. It was explained there are two bathrooms in the teacher’s area where the
ADA coat hook is snagging coats or knapsacks as people exit the bathroom.
There was a request to relocate the coat hooks to an adjacent wall.
14. There was a request to have a phasing meeting with the principal, Suzanne
Morin and Bob Curtis of PDT, as well as Brook and Sheridan Corporation to
better prepare the high school for upcoming construction work done during
the winter and spring.

Meeting Notes

15. PDT has recommended releasing retainage of about $67,000, which is

moving $600,000 on the grass field improvements to the turf and track
project. There is also a proposal to move another $161,182 from
construction contingency to the field at the RSU School Board’s discretion.

take care of the building. The money dedicated for the construction of the
building comes first. If there is any contingency left over, that money may or
may not be given to the turf and track project.

explained there was $650,000 still left.

them somewhere else.

Ouellette will look into getting them removed as early as December 20,
2016.

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
October 5, 2016 at 7:30 a.m.
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5,
Ben Jamo, David Watts/RSU 8, Catherine Breer, Dr. Becky Foley, Superintendent/RSU 5, Charlie
Mellon, Assistant Principal/Freeport High School, David Smail/FHS Physics, Kim LaMarre, David
Steckler, Dennis Ouellete, Facilities Director/RSU 5, Tim Giddings and Michelle Ritcheson.
Purpose: Monthly Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order at 7:35 a.m.
2. Lyndon gave an update/overview of building progress and schedule.
3. The architect and construction team approved Sheridan’s Application for
Payment #11 in the amount of $550,806. This represents 76.8% completion
of the project to date.
4. Change Order Summary #12 dated September 23, 2016 was approved in
the amount of $20,729. It contained seven (7) items which included items
previously discussed and approved by the Building Committee those
included new ceiling and lights at the library office and commons, as well as
new carpet tile in the same area. Also included cracked CMU in Women’s
and Men’s Bathroom 100 which had been previously discussed and
approved.
New proposals approved by the construction team included roof membrane
changes which included tapered insulation and scuppers to be installed at
the “knuckle” between the 1960 building, gymnasium, performing arts center
and the new addition. Other new work included electrical work and added
phone and data lines, as well as infilling an exterior wall vent in Learning
Commons #311.
5. Lyndon passed out a copy of Sheridan’s monthly report, as well as the
monthly summary by Brook Plummer, Clerk of the Works.
6. Lyndon summarized the status of the work saying that a lot of exterior work
had been done over the last month, including base paving, sidewalk gravel
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
sub-base, concrete curbs, spreading of loam and seeding, and final grading
at swales, retention basins as well as general clean up on the site.
7. Lyndon explained that most of the drywall was completed. The ceiling grid
had been installed but the ceiling had not been flooded with ceiling tiles.
Above ceiling inspections will occur the week of October 10th with ceilings
starting to be installed the following week, October 17th.
8. Trico was starting to install millwork and casework and putting benches in
the hallways.
9. A great deal of work had been accomplished on the flooring. Tile floors had
been installed in bathrooms. Tile walls had been installed at the
cafeteria/food court and serving kitchen.
10. Moisture tests for the second floor slab was complete and acceptable
allowing Paul White Flooring to install the remaining vinyl and carpet in the
second floor classrooms.
11. Caprara kitchen equipment will be installed at the end of October and early
November. The cooler/freezer has been installed.
12. Contractor informed the architect and the construction team they were going
to finish the addition early. If the owner wanted it turned over before
Thanksgiving it was a real possibility. The construction team and school
administration will consider whether there are any advantages to taking the
school early. Lyndon explained traditionally schools accept buildings for
occupancy either in the summer or over the holiday break at the end of
December. Generally schools need about 10-12 days to install furnishings
and move teacher’s equipment.
13. There was a question and request that perhaps the music room could be
turned over early and occupied by the school.
14. It was also explained the contractor had asked if they could begin foundation
work for the new front door entrance with ramps and canopy this fall before
the start of winter. It was explained that the construction team and architect
thought this was unlikely because of security reasons. The contractor
suggested letting students and visitors enter the school at the end of the
science wing and back by the new bus drop-off.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 2 of 5

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
15. A question was asked about carbon monoxide fumes and an alarm which
had been set off in the school. It was explained by Superintendent Foley
that new protocol had been developed for Sheridan’s construction
superintendent related to any so-called “hot work” which would involve
welding or combustible devices. This will, hopefully, prevent future carbon
monoxide alarms or fire alarms from accidently being set off by construction
work.
16. There was discussion about whether the contractor could be allowed to
accelerate some other portions of work inside the building. The discussion
included Sheridan’s request that more work be shifted to the winter and
spring to avoid a rushed and chaotic summer renovation schedule.
Sheridan had acknowledged that they had taken on too much work which
resulted in difficult phasing and dusty work conditions.
17. There was a question if the architects had received a revised price for the
bathrooms back by the library and new proposed STEM area. Lyndon
responded that he had not seen the price and would request and forward the
price to John Simoneau as soon as it was received.
18. Lyndon explained that Sheridan Corporation had requested release of
retainage. At present the owner is holding $407,820 of retainage money.
Lyndon explained the purpose for retainage and explained the owner could
withhold all of the retainage until the end of the project, although it was more
common to release retainage about half way through the project if the owner
was satisfied with the quality of the work.
19. Sheridan had requested release of one half of the retainage or $203,500.
20. The Committee agreed to let PDT work with the construction team to
determine which line items would be acceptable for having partial or full
retainage released. It was agreed the Committee would not release a
general percentage on all line items but would look at each line item request
individually.
21. There was a discussion of open ended change proposals which had not
been approved or rejected. It was explained that PDT reviews all change
orders. There were some change orders requested which PDT had rejected
as unacceptable. Those items would fall into a category of doing the work
“under protest” where the contractor could resort to Article 42 for arbitration
and mediation under dispute resolution.

23. There was a discussion of mirrors in the Fitness Room. PDT explained the
specification called for tempered mirrors. The contractor had submitted an
annealed plate glass mirror which was unacceptable to the architect. There
is an on-going discussion between Sheridan as to what kind of mirror should
be installed and what the proper credit or value should be for the installed
mirrors.

landscaping with reinforced turf at the back by the cafeteria. This change

was too late to implement this. The Committee agreed they would not
modify the landscaping plan at the back of the cafeteria at this time,
therefore, rejecting Change Proposal #096R.

benches at the back without affecting the landscaping and grass at a future
time.
26. There was a brief discussion of a request by the Earth Club to consider
electric dyers instead of paper towels in the bathrooms. Lyndon explained
the drawings showed paper towels at the request of the owner. This is a
system the owner uses throughout all of its schools. There are no electric
hand dryers in other bathrooms on the project.
27. Lyndon explained he had not brought the copies of the spreadsheet that
showed improvements the Committee was intending to make in the future.

had chosen to wait on until the winter or spring.

no later than December because of a pending referendum for the three
communities related to the expenditure of monies for track and turf field.
30. John Simoneau gave an update on the track and turf field. He explained a
presentation had been made to the RSU 5 Board. The RSU 5 Board was
considering a referendum in January 2017 to ask the voters to reallocate
bond money and possibly contingency money, as well as the $600,000

Meeting Notes
Freeport High School
Project:
May 4, 2016
Date:
Attendees: Lyndon Keck*, Kim LaMarre/Committee Member, John Simoneau/Committee Chairman,
Dennis Ouelette, Owner’s Representative/RSU 5, Kelly Wentworth, Business Manager/RSU 5,
Brian Campbell/FHS Principal, Ben Jamo, Kevin Nadeau, Seth Thompson, David Watts, Julie
Coleman, Tim Giddings, Ed McDonough, Superintendent/RSU 5 and Suzanne Morin/PDT Architects
Purpose: Building Committee Meeting
These notes were prepared by Lyndon Keck to the best of his ability. If there are any oversights
please notify PDT Architects within three (3) working days.
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
1. The meeting was called to order by John Simoneau at 7:35 a.m.
2. John gave a recap for the Committee of the previous day’s pay requisition
and construction meeting. He explained that the construction team had
approved Pay Requisition #6 in the amount of $469,696. The project is 26%
complete. He also explained Change Order #5, which had five individual
change order items on it, was signed and approved. The total of the five
change order items was $30,066 and included adding the “bright silver” color
to the Marvin windows, providing electrical power and data to the relocated
temporary classroom ($9,436), adding welded wire mesh to hold the radiant
tubing on the first floor ($8,405), constructing a new block pier adjacent to
the Freeport Performing Arts Center ($608), and replacing twelve (12) roof
drains on the existing 1961 roof ($3582).
3. John also explained that Change Order #6 had been approved. It had two
individual change order items on it. The first was changes which needed to
be made to relocate the FPAC HVAC duct ($15,266) and the second change
order item was to insulate the FPAC ductwork where it was discovered it
was not previously insulated ($3,694). Total change order price was
$18,960.
4. John explained that substantial work had happened over the previous month
including the completion of the structural steel bar joists for the floor and
roof, exterior steel stud cold metal framing with dense glass sheathing and
the application of the blue air barrier sheet membrane. John explained that
re-roofing was happening now on the 1961 building. Over April vacation the
waterline had been installed on Holbrook Street with the addition of a new
fire hydrant and new water service into the addition.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 1 of 6

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
5. Lyndon explained that during the course of installing the waterline over April
vacation the contractor had found unsuitable soils on Holbrook Street
containing muck and tree stumps which needed to be removed. This will be
included in a future change order. Lyndon also explained that trench ledge
needed to be removed to install the waterline and will also be part of a future
change order.
6. Lyndon commented that the second floor classroom concrete slab had been
poured the previous month. After the pay requisition meeting PDT had met
with the mason who was starting the brick veneer masonry at the back of the
gym storage areas. Lyndon also explained PDT had selected a tinted color
mortar that more closely resembles the brick color rather than the cheaper
gray white mortar.
7. Lyndon noted the contractor had passed out a revised schedule that showed
overall they were between one and three weeks ahead of schedule on major
milestones of the project.
8. PDT passed out a number of handouts for Committee members. These
included floor plans prepared by Suzanne showing new, existing and reused
furniture for all of the classrooms in the existing building, except for the
science wing; drawings were passed out for the four bathroom conversion
plans at the library, a copy of the agenda, a copy of the April Building
Committee meeting notes, a copy of Brook Plummer’s monthly summary,
and a copy of a matrix developed by PDT for requested building upgrades.
9. The Committee worked its’ way through all of the line items for building
upgrades.
 The first line item was placing the ceiling and lighting in the library.
This item did not have a firm quote from Sheridan Corporation.
Lyndon stated he thought the budget allowance of $98,575 was
probably higher than what the final cost would be. Lyndon estimated
he thought the final cost would be closer to $75,000-$80,000.
 The next line item was replace the flooring in the library with carpet.
A firm price was obtained for this. The Committee chose to take no
action on this item.
 The next item was to gut and renovate the student bathrooms
across from the library. PDT was handed an unfinished quote from
Sheridan Corporation yesterday; it looks like the cost will be in the
vicinity of $100,000. This work can probably be done in the summer
of 2017. PDT needs time to vet the prices quoted by the
subcontractors and see if there are any cost saving measures that
might make sense in the design. The Committee took no action on
this item.

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
 Next was new library furniture. This item has already been included

No action is require.
 Cost for Ethernet cable is included in later items. No action is
required.
 With regard to the classroom IT equipment request, Lyndon
explained in the original master budget ($94,572) had its own line
item for the purchase of devices related to technology. This $94,572
would not come out of construction contingency but out of a

the money. He explained the costs were actually $28,000 less than
what PDT had shown on the matrix. The expected additional cost
over and above $94,572 is $35,246.

as well as existing classrooms in the 100 and 200 wing of the existing high
school. The devices which have been selected are short throw projectors
everywhere in teaching stations. Seth was expecting to purchase the
equipment this summer. It was clarified the vendor would be responsible for
installing the equipment. John expressed concern the vendor’s equipment
install would be compatible with what was left by the electrical subcontractor.
11. The Committee also talked about the electrical IT infrastructure for requested
classroom equipment. This work would be done by Regional Electric, the
electrical subcontractor to Sheridan, who would run conduit and pull wire for
both power and Cat VI cabling.

$25,591 for electrical IT infrastructure to be put in now so future devices can
be installed at any time in the future.
13. The Committee voted unanimously to recommend to the RSU 5 Board of
Directors that an additional $36,246 be allocated out of the contingency for
additional IT equipment devices for the existing classrooms.
14. No quotes were available for exterior seating and tables behind the food
court so there was no discussion on this item.
15. Art installation had been previously approved. John noted the expenditures
to date had been very low. The Art Committee is getting a lot of donated
labor and materials.

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
16. Regarding landscaping and seating in front of the school, there was no fixed

17. There were two types of kitchen upgrades requested. Each approximately
$5,800. The first was Item #14 for stacked washer/dryer with utilities. This
item was requested by the new Food Service Director while the project was
out to bid. The cost is for installation of utility, power, plumbing, and drains.
Questions were asked by the Committee as to whether or not other
washer/dryers could be used within the school. The only other washer/dryer
combo is in the locker room area. PDT stated that Health Departments
generally frown upon kitchen food service laundry being done in equipment
used by locker rooms. Questions were asked about how washing is being
handled now. Brian thought it was either being done in the locker room or
the staff was taking things home and doing it in their own personal
machines.
18. The Food Service Director had also requested other additional upgrades
such as an additional handwashing sink which the construction team had
vetoed.

a request for another table change in the kitchen. This estimate is
approximately $5,800.

to the washer/dryer.
