and upgrades the cash wrap point of sale counters.
22. A Committee member asked about a school sign and if a digital display
would make sense located under or adjacent to the existing school sign in
front of the school. The Committee member suggested the cost could be in
the vicinity of $8,000-$10,000 but power would need to be run out to the
sign. It was suggested the controls for the sign could be wireless.
23. There was substantial discussion with regard to Items #16, #17 and #19
related to proximity readers for exterior doors, security alarms for exterior
doors around the building, additional hardware to support excess control
system, and security cameras, devices and wiring. John expressed concern
that there were still exclusions in the quotations and there wasn’t clarity
about who owned all of the pathways to the doors which needed to be
coordinated with masonry.
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 4 of 6

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
24. Committee members agreed that having the maximum amount of security at
the high school was an important part of the project. The Committee should
carefully review the systems.
25. Lyndon explained that PDT had carried $75,000 in the original construction
budget for security cameras, proximity readers, and door contracts. This
money is intended for the new addition and only for those areas receiving
major renovation, such as the new front entrance, administration, guidance,
STEM classrooms, art rooms and one special education room. No money
was budgeted for the existing building for security systems.
26. There was considerable discussion about Item #19 acknowledging that
pathways need to be put in now but the Committee didn’t feel comfortable
approving or recommending $62,100 for the keyless entries, access control
and security alarms for exterior doors.
27. After additional discussion it was recommended that Item #17, adding
electrified hardware from HCI to support the access control system in the
amount of $20,675 be approved now so the hardware submittal could be
returned to the supplier and the appropriate hardware ordered. The vote
was unanimous.
28. It was also agreed by the Committee that PDT should work with the general
contractor to implement necessary pathways through masonry construction
for new doors as part of Item #19. The Committee did not, however,
approve all of Item #19 for recommendation to the RSU 5 Board.
29. Suzanne Morin asked if any additional furniture expenditures would be
considered at this time. She explained there were items being requested by
Principal Brian Campbell to be spent out of the unspent $40,000 as a result
of the low furniture bid. There was no vote or consensus on this item.
Suzanne also asked if the Committee wanted to consider any part of the
additional $164,600 for additional furnishings at this time. The Committee
said no they want to revisit furniture in the spring of 2017 after the project
had completed most of its interior renovations in the 1961 building.
30. PDT was requested to wrap up all miscellaneous expenses related to the
electrical hardware, access control systems, security cameras, devices and
pathways at least one week before the June meeting so the Committee
would have all numbers in front of them.
31. The Committee also requested the outdoor furnishing quotes be provided at
the next meeting and the cost for the display banner sign and electrical
digital sign be submitted for June as well.
32. Kelly Wentworth presented a summary of the total budget status which
included a three page handout. She explained the project started with two
contingencies. The first is Item 2.5 in the amount of $1,147,739. The
49 DARTMOUTH STREET PORTLAND , MAINE 0 4 1 0 1 2 0 7 – 7 7 5 – 1 0 5 9 WWW . PDTARCHS . COM
Page 5 of 6

Meeting Notes
Topic Agenda/Notes Action
second is the unspent expenditure resulting from the low bids from Sheridan
Corporation.
33. Kelly’s summary clarified there were additional monies that needed to come
out of the unspent Sheridan money which included asbestos expenditures,
portable classroom expenditures, as well as the $75,000 Lyndon had talked
about that was reserved for hardware, cameras, and access control system.
The actual amount of monies available as a result of the unspent low bid
was lower than the $680,000 previously mentioned.
34. There was discussion on the Committee’s part expressing concern that the
so called requested building upgrades were actually a blend which included
items that were not upgrades but had been deletions made in January 2015
as a result of inflation increases due to the lost year before construction.
The $75,000 for hardware is not an upgrade but money reserved for
security. The IT equipment expenditures also have a separate line item for
$94,572. There was an acknowledgement that more traditional change order
items such as unsuitable soils, ledge removal and other discovered items
and omissions were being taken out of the construction contingency line 2.5
under administration costs and reserves.
35. For the next meeting PDT will bring enlarged drawings showing the extent of
cameras, door contacts, and proximity readers for the entire security
package so the Committee has a clear understanding of requested
expenditures.
36. The Committee agreed to let Suzanne work with Brian to make small scale
expenditures out of the $40,000 unspent money as a result of the furnishing
low bid.
37. The next meeting date will be Wednesday, June 1, 2016 at 7:30 a.m. at the
Freeport High School Library. At the end of next month’s meeting
Committee members will go on a tour of the new addition. Hardhats will be
provided. Committee members are to wear boots or closed shoes, no open
toe sandals or high heels.
38. The meeting was adjourned at approximately 9:30 a.m.
END OF MEETING NOTES

Update on 1. PDT reviewed status of work with Town to resolve Sewer District easement
ROW / and ROW issues with the driveway connecting Holbrook St. to Snow Rd.
easement
- Traffic & Parking Committee unanimous approval of site plan
issue
- Next steps include meetings with RSU attorneys and Town Manager
- Ideal progress results in Town deeding a section of the 50’ wide parcel
to the School District with an easement in favor of the Town for access
and enforcement rights over driveway.
- Other options could include revising location of lot lines defining parcel,
but no change in ownership of parcel. This would require a variance for
setback requirements of existing FPAC and new addition.
Update on 2. Efficiency Maine has suspended their rebate program
Efficiency - Restart date is unknown at this time, as is what the program will look
Maine like when it is restarted.
program
- There is time for this project to still make use of potential future rebates.
We wouldn’t submit for rebates until after lights are purchased by the
contractor

- FPAC

Discussion committee for review.

construction plan and get back to Brian
7. Catherine updated committee on her goals for an art sub-committee
- Start meeting this summer, but be more active starting in the fall
8. Committee requested tack boards in the new corridors for announcements,
etc.

a package to class advisor for approval of benches (design and location)

timeline

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Friday, November 3, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: Fred Palmer, Michelle Lickteig, Tim Reich, Kim LaMarre, Rob Ponteau, John
Simoneau, David Roussel, Michelle Ritcheson, Brad Williams, Pat Carroll, John Paterson,
Becky Foley,
Guests: Naomi Ledbetter, Lindsay Sterling, Vali Steverlynck
Updates:

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Friday, October 13, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: John Simoneau, Andrew Johnston, Tim Reich, Brad Williams, Dennis Ouellette, John
Paterson, Becky Foley, Kim LaMarre, Craig Sickels, Fred Palmer, Michelle Ritcheson, Charlie
Mellon, Michelle Lickteig.
Updates:
1. Timeline update:
a. Turf crew should be completed next week.
b. 90% on drainage on the site. Currently working on shaping the south side of the
field, preparing for the donor area.
c. Working on path coming from the Gap parking lot.
d. They’re to subgrade on the softball field.
e. LLBean pole has been moved.
f. Track surface – we have an issue with temperature going forward. There is an
option that we are recommending which includes putting down a base layer
with the Nike Grind. This can then be striped for competitions and they can wait
to lay the topcoat. This allows for the facility to be used this spring. This will be
at no additional cost to the RSU to line the track.
g. June 18-July 13th the topcoat will be installed and the project completed.
h. Most of granite for the donor area has been delivered.
i. We’ve kept 5% retainage in all lines.
2. Budget
a. $124,000 remaining; does not include sod for the softball field.
b. Working on budget for Pat, Brooke, Andrew for the additional costs.
3. Art Update
a. Arch area donors are working with Pat on the design.
b. FALCON PRIDE was the slogan chosen by the students to place on the wall.
c. Communicating with Nike around this to schedule when it will happen.
4. Other:
a. School Board agreed to make the bleachers the priority.
i. All in system would cost $207,000 but doesn’t include concrete pad.
ii. $143,000 is an angle frame bleacher system with press box. Dant Clayton
is a nicer system.
iii. Looking for $175,000 project for the bleachers.
iv. Committee decided to bid out an angle frame bleachers at 500 seats, with
add alt to go to 750, separate add alt for press box, include a filming
platform. Get RFP out in January. System would be ready for fall sports.
v. Concrete pad is needed – at an approximate cost of $47,300.
b. Capacity for seating – Craig shared area districts’ attendance at various sporting
events.
Next meeting: Nov. 3rd

Construction Update
• Contractors doing well

order

• Good shape with construction
• Dugas bought all loam at $5.00/yard and is delivering sand
• Towns did not take any loam because of trucking costs
Contractor Change Request to Retaining Wall
• No cost change
• Retaining wall from large block to smaller block system
• Easier for construction (lighter and does not need heavy equipment)
• Cost is the same
• Structurally they are the same
• Questions about how this would impact the potential for artwork on the wall
• Color will be decided by art teachers
• Motion to move to small blocks passes
Pathway for Morse Street School
• Waiting on town to give us process for making changes to path
Bleachers
• A couple of prices
• Time is running short for a system to be installed by September
• Angle vs Beam system
• Angle frame needs concrete underneath
• No storage
• Concrete underneath
• $200,000 w/o concrete pad
• Contractor can put in pad
• Will give us flexibility
• Might be around $10,000 for pad
• Full system with press box
• Will not be complete by September
• Firm up pricing on pad and the angle frame system

• Softball dugouts
• Existing ones cannot be reused
• Need to stay in line with baseball re Title IX
• Need to explore costs for new dugouts
• Wood frame
• There is a prepped area
• Could be about $20,000 - $30,000
• Will get firmer prices

• Scoreboard base alternate
• Need to discuss where we are with scoreboard
• RFP will be sent out through RSU5

• Has morphed into a semicircular plaza with granite wall and granite pillars
• Will go to tri town board on Wednesday night

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Thursday, March 24, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: John Simoneau , Pat Carroll, Todd Coffin, Andrew Johnston, Tom DeLois,
Michelle Lickteig, David Roussel, Brad Williams, Rob Ponteau, Michelle Ritcheson, Craig
Sickels, Fred Palmer, Charlie Mellon, Kim LaMarre, Becky Foley
Updates:
1. Clarified vote from previous meeting:
Only changed the long jump/triple jump pit in design
2. Ginny is the keeper of all of the documents: the use guidelines, traffic and parking, etc..
3. School Board Meeting on 3/22:
a. Voted on prequalified bidders
i. RJ Grondin (Local)
ii. Shaw Brothers (Local)
iii. Sargent (Local)
iv. Clark Company (Sports related complexes)
v. RAD Sports (Sports related complexes)
vi. Fieldturf Company
b. Board of Directors voted to endorse design as it currently is; talked about the
pathway near the Morse Street School Playground .
c. Endorsed user guidelines; these will need to be submitted to Town Planning.
Three minor changes in the document.
4. Talked about Nike Grind:
a. Nike Grind has quality control and know their product is vetted, which is why
they use Astro Turf. This ensures that there are no other products mixed in.
b. None of the other vendors can certify.
5. Pat – working with town on lighting ordinance
a. Brought in Larry Bartlett who spoke about field lighting
b. Donna is working on ordinance language going to Planning Board
i. Draft ordinance is created
ii. Uses 80 foot poles with LED lights
iii. Ball trackers is being used as this can be a safety issue if the ball goes
above the light; Larry doesn’t feel we need those; we feel we do.
iv. Issue – only 30 foot candle; all other areas are 50 foot candles; we feel the
need for 50 foot candles.
v. Level of candles should be able to be modified as needed for level of play.
vi. Use lower levels for practice; higher for games.
vii. Planning Board will set the light level.
viii. Need higher level of light for recording events also.
c. Had meeting with Donna and Condo owners
i. Abutters signed off on buffering plan
ii. Talked about lighting

d. Met with Donna about move of the long jump; this change was approved without
going back to the Board.
6. Design Changes
a. Morse Street playground – path
i. Adding man-gate: four foot gate; extend and tie into vehicular gate
ii. Can be locked during normal times and open for events
iii. Playground is being redesigned
iv. Will be able to adjust the playground plans
v. Create sidewalk near baseball fence
b. Transition to stabilized base on path, but not to be paved at the moment
i. Build so could be paved in future
c. Add alternate
i. Upgrading around the gap parking lot
7. Budget – 375,000
a. LED lighting – increase of 70,000-80,000; rebate from Efficiency around 15,000;
Resulting in a cost in the $60,000 range
8. Bid projects
a. Add alternates
i. Bleachers
ii. Shock pads
iii. Scoreboard
iv. Donor entry area
v. Lights
vi. Paving of the path
9. Had meeting with Astro Turf
a. Inch and a half pile with Nike infill
b. Shock pads
i. Budget includes cheaper pad Nike shock pad with 1.5 pile
ii. Suggest 3 bids with the different pads
1. NPlast (black one) GMAX – 99 (two life cycle)
2. Brock (speckled) – ASP – GMAX 86 (two life cycle)
3. Brock Powerbase (white waffle) GMAX – 81 (3 life cycle)
80,000 upgrade approximately
10. Track surface
a. Nike will send us a standard to use.
b. 3 bidders:
i. Mondo
ii. Conica
iii. Beynon System -
11. Upcoming Dates:
a. Planning Board – April 5th – 6:00 pm
b. May 3rd Planning Board – 6:00

Unanimous

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Thursday, March 9, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: John Simoneau , Dennis Ouellette, John Paterson, Pat Carroll, Kim LaMarre,
David Roussel, Tim Reich, Andrew Johnston, Michelle Lickteig, Todd Coffin; Craig Sickels,
Tom DeLois, Michelle Ritcheson, Charlie Mellon
Other: Bill Schmidt
Updates:
1. Project Review Board – base project without lights and sound is now approved. It
includes bleachers, and scoreboard.
a. One modification is that we will not start sports events after 6:00 pm.
b. No sound makers such as noise makers are allowed from spectators.
2. DEP is in the process of writing our permit. May be three weeks before we have it
officially.
3. Do we redesign the track with NIKE recommendations?
a. Would need to be reviewed by Project Review Board again to have the
approval have amended.
b. Committee debated pros and cons of changing the design.
i. Decided to keep the current track design, and modify the design to
place the jumping pits outside of the track.
ii. Nike is fine with whatever revisions we make.
4. Believe that we will need to use LEDs with the lighting in order to meet the
requirements.
a. Can adjust the brightness with LED lighting.
b. Efficiency Maine – might qualify for some rebates.
5. Event Management Plan
a. The town’s ordinances require that a permit be obtained for crowds larger than
250
b. The RSU has 202 parking places which is sufficient for groups of at least 600
c. Can accommodate for larger events when utilizing LL Bean lots.
d. We should count the GAP lot as that is a commonly used parking lot
e. It might be a good idea to meet with Police Chief Sue Nourse ahead of time.
6. Update on Neighborhood Meeting
a. Thought the meeting went well.
b. Issues:
i. Buffering around the condo area
ii. Condo residents thought there would be more time for input with
issues with noise.
iii. The abutters, ones directly next to the property, have the most
concerns.

7. Upcoming Dates:
a. March 21st – Traffic and Parking – 7:30 am
b. Planning Board – April 5th – 6:00 pm
c. May 3rd Planning Board – 6:00
d. Town Council meets May 23rd
Next Steps:
a. Pat will speak to Donna about the needed revisions.
b. Some minor revisions need to be made to the guidelines based on last night’s
meeting. This then needs to go to School Board for discussion.
c. March 22nd – go to Board for approval on the guidelines.
d. Any recommended revisions to the Event Management Plan need to be sent to
John Simoneau.
e. Event Management Plan needs to then be sent to the Town.

FHS Track and Field Design Committee
Friday, March 3, 2017
Freeport High School
Minutes
Attendance: Dennis Ouellette, Fred Palmer, John Paterson, Kim LaMarre, Pat Carroll, Andrew
Johnson, David Roussel, Michelle Lickteig, Charlie Mellon, Tim Reich, Todd Coffin, Michelle
Ritcheson John Simoneau, Rob Pontau, Tom DeLois, Craig Sickels, Brad Williams
Other Attendees: Joan Benoit Samuelson, Bill Schmidt
1. Minutes – No issues
2. Updates:
a. Revised submission
i. Throwing events – move to baseball field
ii. Modification of track
iii. Identified 16 handicap parking places that can be utilized. Need eight
for spectator area.
iv. Pulled out visitor bleachers – don’t feel we need at this time
v. With revision, can still have tent area
vi. Pulled lighting from the plan and concessions
vii. Sound – just portable P.A. system is now in the plan; future
permanent system would need to be permitted through the town –
approximate cost of 25,000 which would not include engineering
cost.
viii. Landscape Buffer – 15 foot buffer zone
ix. Property line was flagged:
a. The District owns the trees.
b. Fence line is within 20 feet of our property
3. Project Review Board
a. Will have to go back after ordinance review
b. Will know what cost is by then to know what we could fund such as storage
building, PA system
i. Those approvals may need to be done around August
ii. Considering time limits on amplified sound
1. Concerts at LL Bean end around 9:00 a.m.
2. Permitting goes through police department
iii. Pathway lighting will be done when the other lighting is done
4. Parking and Traffic
a. Chair wants written document beyond the plan
i. Uncertain about submission timeline
ii. March 21st will be next parking meeting at Council Chambers

5. Planning Board
a. Big push was on lighting – Larry Bartlett, consultant for Town
i. We will need to hire our own consultant to assist in our submission
ii. Mostly informational; pole heights.
1. 20 foot is mostly for walkways, not sp
2. Suggesting 30 foot candles; we prefer 50 foot candles – not
sure that would be enough. Want to push stronger.
iii. Town pushing for LED lighting
iv. Town Planner is drafting ordinance criteria
v. Possible timeline:
1. Next meeting April 5th – workshop type meeting
2. Next would be Public Hearing – May 3rd estimation
3. May 23rd – Town Council ordinance revision; will probably take
more than one night
4. June estimation
5. Project Review Board would be next
6. July approval would probably be the earliest possible.
6. Maine DEP
a. They have started to write it; then needs Augusta review and signed. Not
sure it will be here by Wednesday, but can get a letter that approval is on the
way.
7. Design Updates
a. Scoreboard Design: Originally submitted two, but another panel was added,
so it is all on one scoreboard.
b. Reviewed with Nike experts (Ven); they provided feedback
i. Recommend simple curve, instead of broken back.
ii. Allows for largest field space.
iii. One continuous curve – better for runners.
iv. Advantage – long jump, triple jump will be inside of the track; pole
vault would be more isolated which is good.
v. Turf area same; track area is less. Virtually about the same; cost
would be similar; redesign would need to occur.
vi. This will be sent to the consultant to see if there is lighting impact.
1. This could delay construction start.
2. Spend another week looking at this new design.
8. Landscape Update
a. Need to add buffering to lower level
i. Looking at 6’ wood fence
ii. Additional shrubs and trees added
iii. Tim will meet with the neighbors on Sunday to provide updates.
9. Guidelines
a. Still draft form; did not submit traffic plan
10. Track and Field Bidding Process was reviewed. Timeline was distributed. Will go to
Board on March 22nd. Recommendation to the Board.

11. Next Steps:
Ø Construction Team Meeting: invite Kim LaMarre
Ø Ground Breaking Ceremony
o Subcommittee: Kim LaMarre (chair), Becky Foley, Brad Williams,
Maddy Vertenten
12. Upcoming Dates:
a. Project Review Board – March 8th; last on the agenda – meeting begins at
6:00
b. March 21st – Traffic and Parking – 7:30 am
c. Planning Board – April 5th – 6:00 pm
d. May 3rd Planning Board – 6:00
e. Town Council meets May 23rd
Next Agenda:
a. Update from Tim from neighbors
b. Pat Carroll – updated design
c. Special Event Traffic Plan

TITLE: May 13, 2025 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Jean Skorapa, Superintendent of Schools Cynthia Alexander, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Committee
May 13, 2025
7:00 a.m.
https://networkmaine.zoom.us/j/83955767193
Meeting ID: 839 5576 7193
Committee Members: Ashley Schumacher, Cheyenne Farrell, Malik Farlow, Cynthia
Alexander, Jen Gulko, Jean Skorapa
Attendance: Ashley Schumacher, Cheyenne Farrell, Malik Farlow, Cynthia Alexander, Jen
Gulko, Jean Skorapa
Guests: Michelle Milstein, Great Schools Partnership
Absent:
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to
seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.
Intentions do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we
work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and
encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and
avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.
Adopted 11/14/23
RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal: Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.
DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are
respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative
strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community
members.
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928

Minutes
1. Strategic Planning Process Input
a. The district is soliciting input regarding a Portrait of a Graduate as part of the
Strategic Planning Process. The committee provided feedback on the following
questions:
1. What goals, hopes, and dreams do you have for the young people of our
schools?
2. What knowledge, skills, experiences and character traits do you think will
be essential to achieve these goals, hopes, and dreams?
3. In what ways do you think our schools currently help students develop
knowledge, skills, and character traits that you identified? In what ways do
you think our schools help students have the experiences they need to
achieve these goals, hopes, and dreams?
4. In what ways do you think our schools could better prepare learners for
life?
b. The collected information will be included with other data collected.

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”

Gulko, Jean Skorapa
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to seek
accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions do
not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each
other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and avoid the discomfort
around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at
hand.
Adopted 11/14/23
RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal: Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.
DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are respected
and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative strategies that inspire
an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community members.

KellyWentworth,DirectorofFinance&HumanResources ElishaMorris.,DirectorofInstructionalSupport
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Committee
November 19, 2024
7:00 a.m.
https://networkmaine.zoom.us/j/83955767193
Meeting ID: 839 5576 7193
Committee Members: Ashley Schumacher, Cheyenne Farrell, Malik Farlow, Cynthia
Alexander, Jen Gulko, Jean Skorapa
Attendance: Ashley Schumacher, Cheyenne Farrell, Cynthia Alexander, Jen Gulko, Jean
Skorapa
Guests: Jessica Najera (FHS)
Absent: Malik Farlow, Allie Edwards
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to
seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.
Intentions do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we
work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and
encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and
avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.
Adopted11/14/23
RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal: Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.
DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are
respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative
strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community
members.
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

Minutes
1. Group Norms
2. FHS Equity Work
a. Principal Gulko and Freeport High School teacher Jessica Najera shared the
work that is being done by the Freeport High School Equity Team.
1. Barriers for Students - The team is examining “hidden costs” associated
with courses and clubs that may be a barrier to participation.
2. Enhancing Student Voice - The Student Leadership Team is focusing on
peer to peer respect and examining who’s voice is not being heard.
3. Professional Development - Teachers have had professional development
about student microaggressions and their response. They continue to
work on looking at curriculum, instructional materials, and instructional
practices through the lens of equity.
3. Framing the Work
a. Agreed upon definitions for diversity, equity, inclusion and belonging - Making
them front facing for the committee
b. Strategic Planning Process
1. Identifying marginalized groups so all voices are at the table
2. Focus Group for the process
4. Next Meeting: January 14, 2025

● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing judgment.

accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times. Intentions do
not negate impact.

● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and encouraging each

around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the topic at
hand.

a. The committee reviewed the development of the Diversity, Equity and Inclusion
Committee. Initially the committee was an Advisory Committee of the Board. In
2021, the Board of Directors changed the committee’s status to “other”
committee.

preparing a report for the RSU 5 Board of Directors.

“Toinspireandsupporteverylearnerbychallengingminds,buildingcharacter,sparkingcreativity,andnurturingpassions.”
JeanSkorapa,SuperintendentofSchools CynthiaAlexander,AssistantSuperintendentofSchools
KellyWentworth,DirectorofFinance&HumanResources JuneSellers,Ed.D.,DirectorofInstructionalSupport
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Committee
November 14, 2023
Minutes
Submitted By: Jean Skorapa, Superintendent
Committee Members in Attendance: Allie Edwards, Malik Farlow, Colin Cheney, Cynthia
Alexander, Jen Gulko, June Sellers, Jean Skorapa
Members absent: Michelle Tussing, Lily West
Agreed upon Norms
● Trust collective wisdom and give some time and thought to ideas before passing
judgment.
● Be conscious of your own and others’ assumptions, and always go to the source to
seek accurate information.
● Assume good intentions, especially during the busiest and most stressful times.
Intentions do not negate impact.
● Maintain confidentiality concerning students, educators, and families with whom we
work.
● Express a Growth Mindset. We have to support our learning by accepting and
encouraging each other to learn from mistakes as well as from success. Do not try and
avoid the discomfort around difficult conversations.
● Seek out and center the participation or experiences of those most impacted by the
topic at hand.
Adopted 11/14/23
RSU 5 Strategic Plan Goal: Strengthen diversity, equity, and inclusion practices.
DEI Committee Charge: Create a culture in RSU 5 where diversity, equity, and inclusion are
respected and celebrated through the implementation of thoughtful, iterative, and innovative
strategies that inspire an inclusive mindset in RSU5 employees, students, and community
members.
Agenda Items and Discussion:
1. Establish Group Norms
17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

a. The committee reviewed and adopted group norms.
2. DEI “B” - Belonging Discussion
a. The committee discussed adding “B”-Belonging to the committee name and the
importance of a common understanding of the term. Committee members will
bring definitions of the terms diversity, equity, and inclusion to the next meeting
for review.
3. Committee Membership, meeting times, meeting dates
a. Membership - currently there is an open seat for one student, one teacher, and a
member of the Tri-County Equity and Inclusion Committee.
b. Meetings will continue at 7:00 a.m. via ZOOM.
4. Work Plan
a. The committee will discuss the displaying of flags at their next meeting.
5. Upcoming Meetings - via ZOOM
December 12, 2023
January 9, 2024
February 13, 2024
March 12, 2024
April 9, 2024
May 14, 2024

17WestStreetFreeport,ME04032· 207-865-0928

several members were absent.
2. Review Charge - The committee reviewed its charge.
3. DEI “B” - Belonging Discussion - The committee discussed the importance of having
actionable steps and perhaps including “B”as part of the charge.
4. Work Plan - The committee discussed the importance of understanding what is already
in place in our schools. The item will be put on the agenda for the next meeting.
5. Upcoming Meetings - via ZOOM
One committee member graduated and another stepped down from the committee at
the conclusion of last school year. The committee will discuss membership, meeting
times and meeting dates at the next meeting.

TITLE: October 22, 2024 Minutes
TYPE: Finalsite-PDF
============================================================

“To inspire and support every learner by challenging minds, building character, sparking creativity, and nurturing passions.”
Jean Skorapa, Superintendent of Schools Cynthia Alexander, Assistant Superintendent of Schools
Kelly Wentworth, Director of Finance & Human Resources Elisha Morris, Director of Instructional Support
Curriculum and Program Development Committee Report
Committee: Curriculum and Program Development
Meeting date: October 22, 2024
Committee Members in attendance: Danielle George, Maura Pillsbury, Carolyn Jensen
Administrators: Holly Johnson, Will PIdden, Cynthia Alexander
Guests: none
An overview of the year-long curriculum work in science at the 3-6 level was presented to the
committee. The presentation outlined our plan for developing and implementing science units
connected to phenomena-based learning, and alignment to the Next Generation Science
Standards.
The outcomes this year have focused on collective learning and growth through professional
development, implementing phenomena-based instruction, and focusing on the cross-cutting
standards and science practices.
Submitted by: Cynthia Alexander
The next meeting will be held on February 4, 2025.
17 West Street Freeport, ME 04032 · 207-865-0928
